comments:
This report addresses an important practical problem—scalable graph-based semi-supervised learning—by proposing two complementary algorithms: (i) a power-iteration (affine contraction) method that leverages sparse matrix-vector multiplies and parallel implementations, and (ii) a sampling (MCMC / stochastic approximation) algorithm that supports distributed and online operation and tracking of arriving nodes. The paper contains a concise theoretical convergence analysis (contraction argument for the power method and an ODE-based stochastic approximation sketch for the sampling scheme) and presents experiments on real and synthetic graphs (Les Misérables, WebKB, Gaussian mixtures, dynamic stochastic block models) to illustrate accuracy, scalability, and tracking behavior.

Strengths:
- Good motivation: addresses computational and distributional challenges in large graph SSL.
- Theoretical treatment is appropriate for the proposed methods; use of standard stochastic approximation tools is sensible.
- Demonstrates practical properties (distributed implementation, tracking of new nodes) that are of interest for dynamic, large-scale systems.

Weaknesses / issues to address:
- Novelty is incremental: the sampling idea builds on existing reinforcement-learning / PageRank sampling literature; the contribution is the application and empirical study in the SSL setting rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic paradigm.
- Experimental comparisons are limited. The paper compares the two proposed schemes but provides limited quantitative comparison to other established semi-supervised learning baselines (beyond noting σ variants like PageRank/normalized Laplacian). More explicit accuracy/runtime comparisons against standard methods (and wall-clock or communication costs for the distributed sampler) would strengthen the empirical claims.
- Experimental detail and reproducibility could be improved: exact hyperparameter settings, dataset splits, number of labeled nodes, seeds, implementation details (parallel hardware, per-iteration cost) and code availability are not given in sufficient detail.
- The convergence proofs are sketched at a high level; while plausible and relying on standard results, some technical conditions (e.g., irreducibility, bounds on importance-sampling ratios, stability conditions) are only briefly mentioned and could be elaborated.
- Handling of labeled-node departure in the dynamic model required ad-hoc fixes (replacing a departed labeled node); this should be discussed more systematically (implications and alternatives).

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add baselines (e.g., classical label propagation, harmonic functions, more recent scalable graph SSL methods) with quantitative comparisons (accuracy, time, memory, communication) on the same datasets.
- Provide full experimental details and, if possible, release code to facilitate reproducibility.
- Expand the theoretical section to make assumptions explicit (on matrices, step-sizes, importance sampling weights) and, if space permits, provide convergence rates or finite-sample bounds.
- Include analysis or measurements of communication overhead and runtime for the distributed sampler to substantiate claims about scalability.

Overall, this is a solid, well-motivated study with useful empirical illustrations and reasonable theoretical backing. It would be of interest to researchers and practitioners working on scalable or distributed graph learning, but would benefit from stronger empirical baselines and fuller experimental/theoretical detail.
